value.comparison_basis: US daily moves measured vs Tuesday 2026-06-23
value.dominant_driver: crude oil's slide to a four-month low (US-Iran de-escalation and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz) eased inflation fears and pulled US Treasury yields lower across the curve
value.secondary_backdrop: still-hawkish Warsh Fed with the market pricing a second 2026 rate hike, and a US dollar at a 13-month high
value.context: first global macro session after the June 23 tech-led equity selloff

text: June 24, 2026 was a normal Wednesday global macro session; US rate, FX and commodity moves are measured against the prior regular session of Tuesday, June 23, and the official Treasury par-yield CSV read live has its newest row dated 06/24/2026 directly after the 06/23/2026 row. The day's macro tape was set by one dominant cross-asset driver - crude oil's slide to a four-month low, as US-Iran de-escalation and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz eased supply and inflation fears - which pulled US Treasury yields lower across the curve, against a still-hawkish Federal Reserve backdrop (the market now pricing a second 2026 hike) and a US dollar at a 13-month high.

value.shape: bull flattening led by the belly and long end
value.basis_note: the +30 bp figure is the official constant-maturity par-yield basis; Reuters' market-based 2s/10s closed near 25 bp, its flattest since March 2025

calculation.method: read official Treasury par-yield CSV live; 10Y minus 2Y; change vs the 06/23/2026 row

text: US Treasury par yields tumbled across the curve on June 24, closing at 4.11% (2Y), 4.15% (3Y), 4.17% (5Y), 4.28% (7Y), 4.41% (10Y), 4.87% (20Y) and 4.86% (30Y) - down roughly 5 to 10 bp from the June 23 close (2Y 4.16%, 10Y 4.50%, 30Y 4.94%), with the belly and long end leading. That is a bull flattening: the 2s10s slope on the official constant-maturity basis narrowed to +30 bp from +34 bp, and Reuters put the market-based 2s/10s near 25 bp, its flattest since March 2025. The long end kept its small kink, with the 20Y (4.87%) 1 bp above the 30Y (4.86%). Falling yields were the day's macro relief signal, driven by oil's slide easing inflation fears.

value.us_10y_breakeven_note: below the 5y, its lowest since April 2025
value.driver: oil and energy prices tumbling as the Middle East conflict cools and supply routes reopen
value.significance: explains why nominal yields fell even though realized PCE inflation is set to print a three-year high on June 25

text: The mechanism behind the yield drop was collapsing market-based inflation expectations. Reuters reported the US 5-year breakeven inflation rate at 2.20%, its lowest this year, with the 10-year breakeven below that at its lowest since April 2025; one-year euro-area inflation swaps fell back below the ECB's 2% target and the 2-year UK inflation swap hit a six-month low. Falling energy prices as the Middle East conflict cools are pulling forward-looking inflation pricing down rapidly - which is why nominal Treasury yields fell even though realized PCE inflation is expected to print a three-year high on Thursday.

value.brent_basis: Trading Economics CFD June 24; corroborated by AP ~$73.87 and Reuters' four-month-low framing
value.low_marker: four-month low; lowest since late February / since the start of the Iran war
drivers: more tankers moving out of the Strait of Hormuz
US-Iran de-escalation; Trump said Iran sought no transit tolls
UAE exporting at ~85% of pre-war levels (IEA)
value.us_inventory_note: EIA US crude inventories at their lowest since 1984, Cushing below operational minimums

calculation.method: vendor CFD/settlement level with cross-source corroboration; day change vs June 23

text: Crude oil was the engine of the day's macro move, sliding to a four-month low. Brent traded near $73.97/bbl on June 24, down about 4% on the day (Trading Economics CFD; AP put the Brent close near $73.87 and Reuters flagged the lowest level since the Iran war began), with WTI near $70. The fall - roughly 23% over the month and about 40% below the wartime peak - was driven by more tankers moving out of the Strait of Hormuz after US-Iran de-escalation, with President Trump saying Iran sought no transit tolls and the IEA estimating the UAE is exporting at about 85% of pre-war levels. The supply-normalization narrative held even with US EIA crude inventories at their lowest since 1984. Oil's drop is the disinflationary impulse that pulled yields and inflation expectations down.

value.characterization: rose for a sixth straight session to a 13-month high
value.ice_dxy_level_basis: Trading Economics ICE DXY 101.13 at the June 23 close (+0.11%) and ~101.1 in early June 24 trade
value.level_confidence: precise June 24 close carried at medium confidence given vendor/snapshot basis differences
value.driver: hawkish Fed repricing (a second 2026 hike now priced) plus safe-haven demand during the tech rout
value.global_note: Reuters: the dollar's surge is causing less global stress than usual because tumbling oil offsets its inflationary bite abroad

calculation.method: Reuters daily characterization (direction, streak, 13-month high) with Trading Economics ICE DXY level basis; precise close cross-vendor

text: The dollar ground higher again. Reuters reported the dollar index rose for a sixth straight session to a 13-month high, driven by hawkish Fed repricing and safe-haven demand during the week's tech rout; Trading Economics' ICE DXY basis showed the index at 101.13 at the June 23 close and near 101.1 in early June 24 trade, with the June 24 close reported around 101.6 - the precise level carried at medium confidence given vendor and snapshot differences. The strong dollar is the tightening-leaning half of the macro picture, though Reuters noted its surge is causing less global stress than usual because tumbling oil offsets its inflationary bite abroad.

value.fed_funds_pricing: traders adding to bets of a second 25 bp Fed hike by end-December (CME FedWatch); previously a single 25 bp rise was priced
value.fed_stance: hawkish; curve flattening accelerated after last week's Fed statement and Chair Warsh's press conference
value.pce_headline_marker: would be the highest since April 2023
value.forward_view: economists widely expect May to mark this year's inflation peak as lower oil and fading tariff effects curb pressures

calculation.method: FactSet/UBS/BofA consensus for the May PCE release; BEA official prior prints; CME FedWatch pricing

text: The monetary backdrop stayed restrictive. After last week's hawkish Fed statement and Chair Kevin Warsh's press conference, traders added to bets of a second 25 bp rate hike by end-December on CME's FedWatch tool, up from a single rise previously. The next catalyst is Thursday's May PCE price index (released June 25, 8:30 a.m. EDT), the Fed's preferred gauge, which economists expect to rise 4.1% year over year - the highest since April 2023 and up from 3.8% in April (BEA's official series rose from 2.9% in January to 3.8% in April) - with core PCE seen at 3.3%. Economists widely expect May to mark this year's inflation peak, since the very oil decline driving markets today should curb price pressures going forward.

value.rate_marker: highest since September 1995; first hike since December
value.dissent: board member Asada Toichiro dissented, citing downside risks to output and employment
value.summary_released: 2026-06-24 (Summary of Opinions from the June 15-16 meeting)
value.summary_signal: one member said it is appropriate to continue raising the policy rate as underlying CPI approaches 2% and conditions stay accommodative; some members called for faster hikes
value.original_rationale: capping the Iran-war-driven energy shock - now being undercut as oil collapses
value.yen_note: yen languished near multi-decade lows against the surging dollar despite the hike

text: Globally, central banks stayed in tightening mode. The Bank of Japan's Summary of Opinions from its June 15-16 meeting, released Wednesday, affirmed its rate-hike stance: at that meeting the BOJ lifted its policy rate 25 bp to 1.0% in a 7-1 vote (board member Asada Toichiro dissenting) - the highest since September 1995 and its first hike since December - and one member said it is appropriate to keep raising the rate as underlying inflation approaches 2%, with some members calling for a faster pace. The original rationale, capping an Iran-war energy shock, is now being undercut by the oil collapse, yet the yen still languished near multi-decade lows against the 13-month-high dollar, a classic rate-differential and dollar-strength story.

value.gold_spot_marker: slipped below $4,000/oz on a spot basis (Reuters), its lowest this year
value.gold_basis: COMEX future held just above $4,000 (MarketWatch ~$4,018.80, +0.25%) while Reuters spot dipped below - spot-vs-future basis disclosed

vix.note: VIX fell, consistent with risk stabilization

calculation.method: Reuters spot characterization with MarketWatch COMEX future cross-check; month change vs end-May

text: Precious metals were the casualty of the strong dollar and hawkish Fed. Gold hovered around the $4,000/oz mark - Reuters said spot slipped below $4,000 to its lowest this year, while the COMEX future held just above at about $4,018.80 (+0.25%, MarketWatch), a disclosed spot-versus-future basis gap - and is down roughly 12% in June, its worst month since 2008. Silver fell about 8% on the day and is down some 55% from its January peak. The 13-month-high dollar, repricing toward a second Fed hike, and the fading Middle East safe-haven bid drove the metals lower; the VIX, by contrast, fell to 18.63 (-4.4%), consistent with the day's risk stabilization. These daily metals moves are carried at medium confidence.

value.core_tension: a fresh oil-led disinflation impulse (yields and inflation expectations down) against still-hawkish central banks (Fed pricing a second hike, BOJ affirming more) and a 13-month-high dollar
value.key_dependency: the disinflation trade hinges on Middle East de-escalation holding - continued Strait of Hormuz tanker flows and no transit tolls; a flare-up would snap oil and the risk premium back
value.next_catalyst: Thursday's May PCE print decides the rates leg
value.caveat: de-escalation and tanker-flow normalization are evolving and carried at medium confidence

text: Net read: June 24's macro state was a one-way disinflation impulse colliding with a hawkish policy backdrop. Crude's slide to a four-month low pulled Treasury yields down across the curve, flattened the 2s/10s to its lowest since March 2025, dragged market inflation expectations to the year's lows, and sent gold below $4,000 - while the same hawkish-Fed repricing that has the market pricing a second 2026 hike kept the dollar grinding to a 13-month high and the BOJ affirming further tightening. The whole disinflation trade rests on Middle East de-escalation holding: continued Strait of Hormuz tanker flows and Trump's statement that Iran seeks no tolls. A flare-up would snap oil and the risk premium back; absent that, Thursday's May PCE print - expected at a three-year-high 4.1% - decides whether the front end can keep pace with the falling long end. De-escalation specifics are evolving and carried at medium confidence.
